description: Composable AI rule brains for Mauth Studio authoring tasks.
compositionRules: Mauth Studio is designed for AI-controlled authoring first. The in-app assistant should aim for parity with the current Codex-assisted workflow for Mauth document work: question/test authoring, solutions, diagrams, layout repair, file operations, print checks, and validation from inside the app.

Prefer explicit schema fields, structured document actions, deterministic validation, renderer/file inspection tools, and reversible patches over UI-only behaviour an AI cannot reliably inspect or reproduce.

When a requested edit is covered by the Mauth action layer, AI-authored edits should use that structured action instead of arbitrary JSON mutation. Use an atomic action batch for multi-step edits that should apply as one undoable operation, especially front-matter setup, logo/school-name selection, page-format changes, and multipart question creation.

Use module.move, part.move, and subpart.move to relocate existing content while preserving ids, undo/history, mixed itemOrder, and validation; do not simulate moves by deleting and recreating content.

For large AI-authored edits, run a document-action dry run first and inspect the preview summary for validity, touched ids, added/deleted/moved/reordered counts, changed fields, warnings, and validation output before committing the same batch.

For focused requests to write, replace, or append one question, prefer the high-level mauth.question.upsert tool over many low-level module actions. It can replace an existing question or append the immediate next missing question; do not refuse a request for Question 2 just because only Question 1 exists. If the teacher says "add this question" with no explicit number, append the immediate next missing question. Supply questionNumber or questionId, marks, questionText, studentSpaceLines, optional diagram graphConfigs, and solutionText only when a solution is requested or present in the source material.

When a teacher edit request is ambiguous about the target, source, or intended action, ask one short clarifying question before calling an editing tool. Do not guess a question number, invent missing source content, or spend tool rounds on uncertain mutations.

For questions where the answer is completed directly on an artifact, use mauth.question.upsert answerSurface: "diagram" or answerSurface: "table". Pair the student blank/partial diagram/table with solutionDiagram or solutionTable when a solution copy is requested, and do not create a normal answer-space block unless written working is also required. The authoring layer places red solution ticks on the completed solution surface, so do not duplicate those same marks in following solutionText. A small safety inference catches common complete-table and sketch-grid wording when answerSurface is omitted, but assistant payloads should still be explicit.

Do not add worked solutions merely because a question has marks. For screenshot/PDF conversion prompts that ask for questions only, omit solutionText, parts[i].solutionText, and includeSolution unless the source visibly includes solutions.

For focused follow-up requests to add a diagram to an existing question, prefer the high-level mauth.author.addDiagram tool over low-level module patches. Choose the renderer first, then emit a real graphConfig for that renderer. Use diagramId on repair calls when validationIssues identify an existing diagram to replace. Do not use standardDiagram recipe names for assistant-authored diagrams.

When using high-level diagram fields on mauth.question.upsert or mauth.author.addDiagram, wrap the renderer payload exactly as { graphConfig: { type: ... } }. Do not place type/data/options directly on diagram, and do not use config as a shortcut name.

Choose diagram renderers from intent before writing graphConfig: geometricConstruction for schematic circle/tangent/chord theorem geometry, graph2d for coordinate/function graphs, vector2d for coordinate/component vectors on axes and source-faithful no-axis scalar-product/vector-ray diagrams, statsChart for histograms/column/probability charts, setDiagram for Venn diagrams, network for networks, graph3d for 3D, and image only when a bitmap is genuinely intended.

The high-level assistant authoring boundary performs deterministic diagram-intent validation for obvious classroom patterns. If it returns a validationIssue on arguments.diagram.graphConfig.type, switch to the expected native renderer and emit that renderer's schema instead of trying to force the original graphConfig through.

After creating or repairing a diagram, use mauth.preview.inspect when available and read question.diagrams[].warnings plus rendered preview warnings. The frontend adapter also performs this post-edit check for high-level diagram tools after waiting for the preview to repaint when mounted, and returns repairable validationIssues before any success message if warnings remain. Repair wrong renderer choices, missing image sources, missing scalar-product vector labels or angle markers, statsChart probability/relative-frequency mistakes, missing named vector2d metadata, missing requested setDiagram shading/counts, rendered diagram failures, likely label collisions, clipped/page-fit warnings, missing L-shaped answer-space outlines, and Penrose circle-geometry mismatches before telling the teacher the diagram is correct.

When a successful diagram-bearing edit returns semanticReview.required, compare the teacher request, the focused question text/module previews, and the actual diagram summary before saying the edit is done. Use model reasoning over the compact artifact: if the question describes two straight lines but the graph2d summary contains a quadratic, or if the diagram otherwise does not represent the task, repair with the focused high-level tool. Do not grow thousands of narrow deterministic prompt-specific rules when a compact semantic review can judge the question/diagram match.

After mauth.author.ensureSolutions, mauth.author.adjustResponseSpaces, mauth.format.apply, or a mauth.question.upsert with solution/response-space payloads, the frontend adapter also waits for preview repaint and runs post-edit inspection. Repair student-space-missing, solution-hidden-mark-total-mismatch, solution-visible-mark-note, rendered-solution-space-overflow, rendered-response-space-outline-missing, rendered-page-overflow, graph/question mismatches, and formatting-layout warnings once with the matching high-level tool before telling the teacher the layout, question, diagram, or solutions are done.

For focused solution-writing requests where the compact summary already contains enough question text, prefer the high-level mauth.author.ensureSolutions tool. It should create or resize the matched student answer space and add solution-only text without spending extra provider rounds just to inspect ids or module counts.

For whole-test solution-key requests, prefer mauth.solutions.writeAll. It must include solution payload coverage for every marked question, part, and subpart, preserve shared diagrams, create/resize matched student spaces, validate hidden [[marks:n]] totals, and review the returned layout check before reporting completion.

For broad layout, solution-fit, missing-answer-space, weird-blank-page, oversized-diagram, or print-readiness requests, use mauth.layout.check in student, solutions, or both mode. It is inspect-only; repair the returned issues with the narrow high-level tool that owns the problem.

For focused answer-space or working-space edits that do not ask for solution changes, prefer the high-level mauth.author.adjustResponseSpaces tool. It should resize or add student-only response spaces for existing questions, parts, or subparts while preserving wording, diagrams, solution modules, ids, and ordering.

For focused formatting/layout edits that do not ask for content changes, prefer the high-level mauth.format.apply tool. Use it for page breaks before questions/parts/subparts, diagram alignment, answer-space line tweaks, module moves, solution-fit requests, and conservative spacing tidy while preserving wording, diagrams, solutions, ids, and ordering.

Solution authoring tools use hidden [[marks:n]] annotations at the end of mark-worthy solution text lines, and solution table/diagram surface ticks for completed artifact-answer surfaces, to render red check marks. Do not put visible [1 mark] or (1 mark) notes in solution prose.

In-app assistant work should use the assistant tool loop: mauth.document.inspect, mauth.actions.preview, mauth.validation.run, mauth.actions.apply, then commit the returned document through editor history/autosave. Do not teach AI workflows to mutate React state, DOM nodes, browser cache, or raw project files directly.

If mauth.actions.preview or mauth.actions.apply returns validationIssues, repair the listed field paths and retry the same tool call once before explaining the failure. Treat paths such as actions[0].scope.questionId, actions[0].blocks[0].lines, actions[0].patch, actions[0].blocks[0].graphConfig.data.chartType, or actions[0].patch.graphConfig.metadata.vector2d.vectors[0].components[1] as schema errors in the emitted Mauth action payload, not as teacher-facing problems.

For diagram actions, emit one of the supported graphConfig.type values only: graph2d, vector2d, graph3d, image, geometricConstruction, network, setDiagram, or statsChart. The action validator checks renderer-specific diagram fields, so prefer explicit structured fields over invented diagram JSON.

In-app assistant file operations should use mauth.files.* tools for list/open/save/save-as/create-folder/duplicate/rename/move/delete/version operations. The chatbox adapter must still update active editor state and autosave drafts after file tools return.

If a mauth.files.* call returns validationIssues, repair the listed argument paths and retry the same file tool once before explaining the failure. Treat paths such as arguments.path, arguments.paths[0], arguments.content, or arguments.versionId as schema errors in the emitted file-tool payload.

Project-file saves must preserve revision safety. When saving the active file, use the revision loaded with that file; do not fetch the latest revision immediately before saving just to overwrite it. If a file changed on disk, reload it or save the current draft as a copy.

File-save and autosave conflict handling should be quiet infrastructure. Prefer background save/reload/refresh/revision-sync steps when they are safe, keep teacher-facing messages short, and ask only when a real data-loss choice remains.

When Codex, scripts, or backend AI edit a project file that may also be open in Mauth, they must keep the project-file API metadata, active revision, and autosave draft consistent. Do not create a workflow where the browser autosave can silently restore an older draft over a freshly edited project file.

Visible chatbox/provider code should call runMauthAssistantAdapterTool so model tool calls pass through one host boundary for document commits, file parsing, file saving, active-file updates, and user-visible changed id/path logs. The Assistant panel chat provider and manual JSON runner both use that adapter; future provider work must build on that path instead of inventing a parallel mutation route.

Mauth actions and validation are assistant/editor internals, not teacher-facing chores. The assistant/provider workflow should preview and validate generated action batches in the background, apply accepted actions through the normal editor history/autosave path, and explain the outcome in plain language.

When the assistant cannot do a Codex-level document task because it lacks context, add an inspection tool, validator, action type, diagram repair tool, attachment tool, or brain rule. Do not solve repeat gaps by teaching the model to guess hidden UI state or emit raw JSON patches.

Whole-test format is document-level state under T. Use formatting.update and pageFormat.update for title-page/test-structure settings such as presets, mark visibility, page size, margins, and page-break-label visibility; use module-specific actions for question content, diagrams, tables, charts, answer spaces, and solutions.
Use only the brains needed for the current task.

Optimise assistant API cost only after the relevant reliability gates are green. Prefer native deterministic fast paths for status/layout/file checks, referenced-question document summaries, selected brain packets, narrow focused tool schemas, cached stable schema/brain context, local completion messages after successful validation, and no-cost self-smoke tests before paid live evals. Do not reduce context in a way that makes the assistant guess, skip validation, or avoid semantic review.
All brains must operate on the shared Mauthdown/test schema.

Any brain that emits LaTeX must follow the Formatting Brain's MathJax style contract: use $...$ for inline maths and $$...$$ for display maths, never \(...\) or \[...\]. Inline maths is display-style by default, but large nested fraction numerators or denominators still need explicit \displaystyle on the nested expression.

When converting source exams, preserve the original inline/display math distinction. Tall inline vectors, matrices, or equations stay inline if they are part of a sentence; display math is for standalone source equations or deliberate working lines.

When converting source exams, preserve meaningful source line breaks and paragraph breaks. Text modules may contain real newlines; do not merge separate source lines into one long line unless the original layout break was clearly accidental.

Use supported MathJax environments such as \begin{aligned}...\end{aligned} for vertical systems. Do not emit \begin{aligned*}; add small row gaps such as \\[2pt] only when vertical equations need more breathing room.
Question wording must not contain formatting, diagram, or solution-only decisions.
Formatting, diagram, and solutions changes must preserve question intent unless the user explicitly asks for content changes.
When brains disagree, preserve mathematical correctness first, then printable clarity, then editor convenience.
Update a brain only when a rule should affect future authoring, not for a one-off current-test preference.
When a durable brain rule changes, update the human documentation in docs/ at the same time.
